speaker: interviewee
text: I have to say that wherever in which country I was or in which place I was, my-- the one contribution I can make is talk about the dignity of every person, also regardless of age.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK. I've usually been asked to come because I was teaching social group work or I was asked to come as a consultant for institutions, usually also as a speaker. My last two speech-- big speeches, I think, one was in Canberra, in-- in Australia. That was-- That's quite a while ago. That was the year of the child. I started at the university, The Center For Youth Development and Research. So it was related to adolescents and about young people.

speaker: interviewee
text: The University of Minnesota I started that. But then, as I say, I got often asked about talking about adolescents, young people. And that was in Canberra. And the last one I gave I think is about five years ago in Hong Kong. And that was talking about international aspects of working with youth. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's a lot of looking at young people. I enjoy adolescents. I think they are maligned. So I want that people understand them better. And some people know that I retired. And Paul died in 1976. This was catastrophic. And I had to come out of something. And I always said the only thing that will bring me out is to be considered-- continue have a cause. And in many ways, the cause continues, being-- speaking for young people, speaking for the people who cannot speak for themselves. That's speaking for. But then also working with people. And I thought-- I think it's also '76-- oh, I'm getting a little mixed up-- that I also retired, quote, from the University of Minnesota. Oh, meanwhile, I wasn't only an assistant professor. I was a full professor. What I-- I'm not-- It hasn't ended. At the medical school is the adolescent health unit. And the person who should get all the credit is Dr. Robert Blum who once worked for me, but that's when he was young. [LAUGHS] And he's very special and is known now all over the world. And I never forget when I said something about the center will maybe go. You know how things go. He said, oh Gisela, the phoenix will come out of the ashes. And there he is. I'm still attached to that place. I'm asked to consult with them. I teach fellows from psychologies-- psychology, medicine, social work, and so on. That I still do. But the major concern, really, is with people in the community. And I helped a little bit in St. Paul with their neighborhood thing. I think-- I don't usually like to read this. Lots of poetry I wish to read, but I won't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you do, if I could ask you, is there anything that you would like to say as legacy--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I want to do--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I want to say. I was-- There are two different things, totally-- not separate. I was recently at an exhibit-- that's just last week-- of Jacob Lawrence, who is a wonderful black artist. And I saw a tape. And somebody asked him whether he always wanted to make a political statement in his pictures. And his wife answered, and she said something like this. 'He never said I want to make a political statement. Yet, all his work and all his life was such a statement.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And I somehow would like that this is my life. That somehow it's a statement. It isn't that I'm professor. What difference does it make? I could be still a very good cleaning woman, though maybe my knees aren't so good anymore. But it's what I want to say. So I thought I only read two statements, and they are not too long. One is that once asked me what I wanted to say to summarize about what I think about my life. How can one do that? I can't.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I said, perhaps I have had the fullest life one can expect. I have experienced degradation, scorn. I have been spat upon by an arrogant Nazi who considered me a member of an inferior race. I have known fear and hunger and cold and utter despair. I have received unending love by a man who has always been my friend, my protector, lover, companion. I've known the sweetness of children's arms around me, though I had not borne them. I knew and know the gentle friendship of young and old people and the exhilaration of standing up for my ideas. Even in the face of terrible rejection, I have had and have the privilege of knowing many people of great variety, of all races and nationalities, men and women. And that I wrote to them, and I still think it's all right. They still put it in [INAUDIBLE], which is OK with me. But once-- and that is not so long ago-- they have asked me often to talk to people about the Nazi period and especially also to some younger people. And there, I had-- finally I had to end something, and I want to read that. No talk about the Holocaust, no memorial should ever end only with the memory of the dead and tortured. So they must be honored with all our hearts. It should and must always end with a thorough denunciation of the evil of race superiority of any kind, the thorough rejection of the absurdity of the myth and conceit of placing any group over another. Whether it be a religion, gender, nation, race, all of us must take an active stand against the horror of those. And we must actually do something in addition, and that is work on friendship and appreciation of a variety of people. And I like to quote Alan Paton, who in South Africa once said, 'If people do not touch each other, they will hate each other some day.' So I think we must touch each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Andreas-- Andreas will tell you the directions. He'll give you the directions [INAUDIBLE]. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you can tell me who is in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately in 19-- I follow this. Is that all right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately 1916. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is in front of our store in Berlin, Nachod Strasse, N-A-C-H-O-D. And then the people in the photo is to the left, my sister, Hanna. Then the little one is my sister, Ruth. And the other one is myself, Gisela. And I don't know who took the picture. And these photos were in the possession of my mother, and when she died-- she died in Israel-- my sister sent me some of the photos. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't cut our heads off. [PAUSES FOR 71 SECONDS] OK. Go ahead. Tell me about this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately 1914 or 1915. The place- it's a photographer in Berlin. And the names of the people. To they left, that's myself, Gisela. Then comes my mother, Bronia. And then my sister, Hanna. And I don't know who took it. Probably a photographer. And the same way I got those pictures later from my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, but I would think 1935, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Berlin. My mother, Bronia Peiper. I cannot say who took the picture. And, again, I probably got all this later from my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, Mendel Peiper. And, oh dear. In Berlin. Oh, the date. I would think something like 1930 approximately. And this is a drawing that was made by somebody whom I don't know. And I got this, again, later when my mother sent me material. [PAUSES FOR 36 SECONDS] Probably 1935, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Berlin. Ruth Peiper, my sister. I don't know who took the photo. And it's possible that she sent me that while I was in France. No. I don't really know.

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- Again, I think 1935. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think Tel Aviv, Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hanna Weil, W-E-I-L, my older sister. And I do not know who took the photo. And I assume she sent that to me when I was in the-- later when I was in the United States. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I'm never sure of the date. It could be 1940. And from the left, it's my brother-in-law, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Weil. I can't think suddenly of the name. Maybe later. My sister, Hanna Weil. The little boy's name-- is of friend of my nephew's, so I don't know. And the other one is my sister's son, Chanoch Weil C-H-A-N-O-C-H. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Who took it, I don't know. And I don't know how they sent it to me. It's in Tel Aviv, Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: The person on the left. The man.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942 or 1943. In the American Army. I don't know where it was. And this is Paul Konopka, my husband. And I don't know who took it. And he sent it to me when he was in the Army. [PAUSES FOR 27 SECONDS] 1918 or 1919 or 1920. I can't be sure. The place I do not know. The name of the person is Henryk, H-E-N-R-Y-K, Glaser, G-L-A-S-E-R. I don't know who took it. It comes, again, from the photos that my mother had.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me who we're looking at.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could it-- Gisa, who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I didn't know. 1937. And I don't know. Either Berlin or Vienna. One of the two. And it's Gisela Konopka, myself. And I don't know who took it, and I don't really know how I get all these. [PAUSES FOR 24 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: No. What's that? [INTERPOSING VOICES] She's speaking something about that I just can't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me who is in the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I have to think for a minute. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] 1940. No. Well, yeah, probably 1940. The place is in France. And it's Gisela Konopka, myself. And Paul took it, Paul Konopka. And that maybe it was in my possession. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] You'll never [? guess. ?] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 19-- I don't know. I'm not sure. 1940, perhaps. And I think it was in France. And the left is Ruth, my younger sister. And to the right is myself, Gisela Konopka. Oh, my younger sister is Ruth Peiper. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't know how I get these pictures. I really don't.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Gisela and Paul Konopka. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Who took it? Some newspaper. And we got it from the newspaper.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. This was the first communication we got from Germany after the war from people who had been in prisons or concentration camps. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] 1976. Arizona. Paul and Gisela Konopka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't know who took it. And we took it home from Arizona. And this was the last trip I had with Paul before he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead and tell me what this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my birth certificate in Berlin. And I'm born-- should I read that? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: They can read it. But I'm February 11, 1910.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gisela, how did you come by your birth certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I brought that with me. I don't know how I got it, but I must have had it in France finally.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why don't you tell me what this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's the removal of my German citizenship because we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how do you call it? There's a word for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It's a removal of my German citizenship because my parents and we children were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had become German citizens in 1919. So we certainly were born-- the children were born in Germany-- but my parents did come originally form Austria. So-- Then the Nazis removed that citizenship according to their law because we were Jews. [PAUSES FOR 33 SECONDS] We had been naturalized citizen. I was looking for that word. Naturalized citizens. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] And it says there that it cannot be recalled. I mean, it has to stay according to the Nazi law. But-- I don't-- I don't know the year anymore. They have to look that up. It must be there somewhere. But it just--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1937 In January. It says that I have the right to reside in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get this paper?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get this paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Vienna. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It says, you know, mayor of the city of Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's a permission to travel in France. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I think it's from Montauban to the United States. I have to look at that. Let's see. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The course, from Portugal. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] It's given by the police department of Montauban. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In March in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me what this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the paper instead of a passport which when I came to the United States that was given by the council in Marseilles and signed by it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 1941. [PAUSES FOR 33 SECONDS] This is about the worst picture. I wonder what we ever did to this hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you can tell me-- just say this is the back of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the back of the document to which I came to the United States instead of a passport. [PAUSES FOR 19 SECONDS] It shows that I was first in Portugal. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's like something that you get put into your passport when you go to different countries. [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS] This is actually my last book. I have written many others. But this is the story of Paul and myself from the time I was born to the time we enter the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Published in 1988. And it will be now also published in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] The drawing is a woman behind the barbed wire. And the hands is a sculpture that Paul did of different ages reaching into each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: This jar was given to me by the city of Bremen. When I spoke there right after the war, Kaisen, K-A-I-S-E-N, was the mayor who was all during the Nazi time, very opposed to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have a whole map of drawings done by this extraordinary artist. His name was Fritz, F-R-I-T-Z, Lederer, L-E-D, as in Dorothy, E-R-E-R. And--
